titleStmt.title: Letter from Robert Brown to Matthew Nathan, 15 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Robert Brown

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Robert Brown to Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939). In this letter, Brown discusses the case of Henry McCambley (b. 1871), who was accused of fraud, and stated that he has yet to be fully compensated for his attendance of the trail. He goes on to state that he was surprised at his treatment by officials regarding the case.Sir Matthew Nathan was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5613/6824

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
The National Margarine Company
Limited 10, Quality Street, Leith 15th. April 1916
To The Under Secretary, Dublin Castle, DUBLIN. Dear Sir
Re H McCambley-Alleged Fraud.
In accordance with a writ issued on 26th.day of Oct.1915 (copy of
which I enclose you) I proceeded to Belfast on Saturday 30th.Oct.
in order to attend the Court held at Belfast at 11a.m. on 1st.
Novr, returning home on 2nd.Novr.
My expenses in connection with the journey including less of
remuneration through absence from business, and claimed by me, amoun
âted to Â£3/7/11. The sum of Â£2/10/0 was handed to me,by but evidently
the balance is not forthcoming. As it is now over five months since
I sustained the loss remuneration through absence from my work
I shall be glad if you will give instructions for payment of the
balance due to me without further delay.
I cannot refrain from expressing my surprise at the treatment meted
out to me by the Officials in co nection with this case. Yours truly, Ron Brown
Assistant Manager
